g.data-narration: Welcome to Geometric Transformations in Image Processing. This module covers how we change pixel positions in images.
Geometric transformations alter spatial relationships between pixels while preserving their values, unlike pixel value modifications.
g.text: Operations that change the spatial positions of pixels while preserving their
intensity values. Unlike point operations (modify values) or neighborhood
operations (change based on neighbors), geometric transformations move pixels.

g.data-narration: These transformations power image registration, camera calibration, perspective correction, and panoramic stitching applications.
g.text: Essential for: Image Registration | Camera Calibration | Perspective Correction
Image Morphing | Panoramic Stitching | AR Applications

g.data-narration: We map between spatial domain (x,y) in the original image and transformed domain (u,v) using mapping functions.
g.text: Original image uses spatial coordinates (x,y). Transformed image uses (u,v).
u = f(x, y) v = g(x, y)

g.data-narration: Forward mapping moves source to destination pixels but creates holes. Inverse mapping is preferred as it avoids gaps and overlaps.
g.text: find source pixel (x,y) to sample from.

g.data-narration: Translation shifts every point by fixed distances tx and ty, preserving the image shape and size completely.
g.text: Shifts every point by a fixed distance (tx, ty) in x and y directions.

g.data-narration: Rotation rotates points around the origin by angle θ using cosine and sine functions, preserving distance from origin.
g.text: Rotates points around the origin by angle θ (counterclockwise positive).

g.data-narration: Scaling changes image size by factors sx and sy. Values greater than 1 enlarge, values between 0 and 1 shrink the image.
g.text: Changes size by scaling factors sx (horizontal) and sy (vertical).

g.data-narration: Shearing slants the image shape using shear factors shx and shy, creating italic-like effects while preserving area.
g.text: Slants the shape using shear factors shx (horizontal) and shy (vertical).

g.data-narration: Affine transformations preserve parallel lines using 3×3 matrices. Projective transformations allow perspective effects with 8 degrees of freedom.
g.text: Maps lines to lines, not parallel. 8 DOF.

g.data-narration: Interpolation estimates pixel values at non-integer coordinates. Nearest neighbor is fast but blocky, bilinear is balanced, bicubic is smoothest.
g.text: When transforming, source coordinates often fall between pixels. We need interpolation.

g.data-narration: Real-world uses include aligning medical scans, correcting lens distortion, stitching panoramas, and document scanning.
This table summarizes transformation types: translation has 2 DOF, affine has 6, projective has 8. Each preserves different geometric properties.
Remember: Geometric transformations change pixel positions, use inverse mapping for best results, choose interpolation based on speed vs quality needs.
g.text: • Geometric transformations modify spatial relationships between pixels, not pixel values
• Use inverse mapping to avoid holes and overlaps in output images
• Affine transformations preserve parallelism; projective allows perspective effects
